results.possibleIdentity: Giselle Mauer was a Romanian-born sculptress active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is historically recognized as a participant in Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a New York-based non-profit organization founded in 1966 to foster collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists. Mauer was involved with E.A.T.'s Technical Services Program, which launched in 1967 to provide artists with access to new technology and technical collaborators. Her work was included in early E.A.T. initiatives, possibly including the landmark 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum, where she was among a group of women artists whose contributions were noted despite a general lack of detailed biographical records in historical archives. In May 1972, she unveiled a modern metal sculpture at the Hillel Center on Ryders Lane in New Jersey. Additionally, in early 1966, she was involved in an art project that involved collecting items through the cooperation of ambassadors and school teachers.

results.possibleIdentity: Nicholas Roukes is an American-born artist, author, and art educator who was significantly active during the late 1960s and 1970s and continued his career into the early 21st century. Born and raised in San Jose, California, he earned degrees from California State University, Fresno, and Stanford University. He eventually became a Professor Emeritus of Art at the University of Calgary in Alberta, Canada. Roukes is best known for his pioneering work and instructional publications on modern art materials and techniques, particularly the use of plastics and acrylics in sculpture and painting. His notable books from the late 1960s and 1970s include 'Painting with Acrylics' (1966), 'Crafts in Plastics' (1970), 'Sculpture in Plastics' (1972), and 'Plastics for Kinetic Art' (1974). His focus on kinetic art and the application of industrial materials align with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which sought to bridge the gap between artists and engineers. In his later career, Roukes became a prominent advocate for 'visual humor' and 'synectics' (a creative problem-solving technique involving unusual associations), authoring books such as 'Art Synectics' (1982), 'Design Synectics' (1988), 'Humor in Art' (1997), and 'Artful Jesters' (2003).

results.possibleIdentity: Alfonso Alfonso González is a Cuban architect and interior designer who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is specifically noted for his work on the Pavilion of Cuba at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, where he designed the interiors and created a 1:3 scale model of the pavilion. This period and location coincide with the height of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which was simultaneously producing the famous Pepsi Pavilion at the same Expo. He has served as a professor at the University of Havana and has a background in technical sciences and design, including work on early Cuban computer prototypes (CID 201).

Alfonso Gonzalez Sr. (also known as AL SIGNS) is a sign painter and commercial artist who moved to Los Angeles in the early 1970s. Originally from Tijuana, he has maintained an art practice for over 40 years, focusing on hand-painted signage, truck lettering, and outdoor advertising. His career and technical skill in sign painting served as the primary influence for his son, the contemporary artist Alfonso Gonzalez Jr. (born 1989), whose work frequently references the urban signage and working-class culture of East Los Angeles. While primarily a commercial artist, his work in professional lettering and signage represents the intersection of craft and visual communication active during the 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Martin Cohen (1945–2021) was an American visual artist and filmmaker based in New York City, active in the conceptual, video, and performance art scenes of the late 1960s and 1970s. His work included screenprints and video, such as the piece 'Another Monologue in Search of a Dialogue' (1980), which was featured in the Museum of Modern Art's (MoMA) retrospective of the 'Just Above Midtown' (JAM) gallery. Cohen was a student at SUNY Purchase in the late 1960s, where he experienced a psychiatric crisis that later informed his artistic practice and advocacy. He was a frequent exhibitor at alternative New York venues including Artists Space (where he participated in a 1979 installation) and The Kitchen (where he was included in the 1981 exhibition 'Pictures and Promises' alongside artists like Robert Rauschenberg and Cindy Sherman). In 2000, he co-founded the Fountain House Gallery, a premier venue in New York for artists living with mental illness.

Martin Cohen (born January 28, 1939) is an American mechanical engineer, inventor, and photographer who founded Latin Percussion (LP) in 1964. While primarily known as an entrepreneur in the music industry, Cohen's career aligns with the collaborative spirit of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. He graduated with a degree in mechanical engineering from the City College of New York in 1961 and worked for companies such as Bendix and Airborne Instruments Laboratory. Cohen utilized his engineering background to collaborate with legendary New York Latin and jazz artists to design and patent innovative musical instruments, such as the Vibra-Slap. He is also a prolific photographer and documentarian who has captured thousands of musical performances and artist portraits through his 'Congahead' project. His work frequently intersected with the New York avant-garde and experimental music scenes of the 1960s and 1970s.

Martin Cohen was an art critic and writer active in the late 1960s. He is notably recognized for his early coverage of the Japanese avant-garde art movement, specifically the Gutai Group. His article 'Japan’s Gutai Group,' published in Art in America in 1968, served as a significant introduction of the group to Western audiences at a time when major figures in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, such as Robert Rauschenberg and John Cage, were interacting with the group and exploring international art-technology collaborations.
